disease.name: Pediatric Bronchiolitis

ageGate.hardRule: If age >24 months, reduce bronchiolitis probability significantly. If age <1 month, consider neonatal sepsis/pneumonia.

etiology.note: Respiratory Syncytial Virus dominates in pediatric population

seasonalModifier.description: Typical RSV season (rainy/cold months) increases bronchiolitis prior probability.

historyFeatures.note: Usually low-grade; high fever reduces bronchiolitis likelihood in pediatric population.

historyFeatures.description: Feeding less than 50% of normal is a strong severity indicator in infants.
Profuse rhinorrhoea/congestion is the typical prodrome.
Apnoea can be the presenting feature in young infants, especially preterm <3 months.
First episode of wheeze in infancy strongly favours bronchiolitis over asthma.
Coryza, low-grade fever, cough for 1-3 days before onset of respiratory distress.

examFeatures.note: Expiratory wheeze, often bilateral, hallmark of bronchiolitis.
Diffuse fine end-inspiratory crackles are classic.
Less common than in pneumonia, but indicates severe distress in infants.
Severe sign in infants indicating respiratory failure risk.

westleyScore.note: Adapted Westley Croup Score concepts not used here. Bronchiolitis severity assessed by modified clinical scoring evaluating wheeze, indrawing, SpO2, general condition, and feeding ability.

immediateDangerSigns.note: Apnoea is a presenting feature in RSV bronchiolitis, especially in young/preterm infants.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Feeding less than 50% of normal or completely unable.

riskFactors.note: Especially <32 weeks gestation, chronic lung disease of prematurity.
Hemodynamically significant CHD increases severe disease risk.
Higher risk of apnoea and severe disease.
Increased risk of severe RSV bronchiolitis.

laboratory.name: Nasopharyngeal aspirate for RSV/rapid viral panel
laboratory.priority: if cohorting needed or diagnostic uncertainty
laboratory.indication: outbreak control, avoid unnecessary antibiotics, cohorting in hospital

laboratory.name: FBC + CRP
laboratory.priority: low; only if toxic or suspected bacterial superinfection
laboratory.note: Often non-specifically elevated in viral illness.

laboratory.name: Blood culture
laboratory.indication: only if fever >39°C, toxic appearance, or concern for bacterial co-infection

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.indication: apnoea, severe disease, diagnostic uncertainty, asymmetrical signs, failure to improve
imaging.findings: Hyperinflation, peribronchial cuffing, atelectasis. Patchy infiltrates possible. NOT consolidation unless secondary pneumonia.

management.protocolReference: Kenya Paediatric Guidelines + AAP 2025/NICE 2024 bronchiolitis guidelines + WHO principles

pathways.criteria: Feeding well (>75% normal), no respiratory distress, SpO2 ≥95% on room air.
treatment: Nasal saline drops and gentle bulb suction before feeds
Small frequent feeds to maintain hydration
No bronchodilators — no evidence of benefit in bronchiolitis per latest guidelines
No corticosteroids — not recommended
No antibiotics unless proven bacterial co-infection
Counsel parents on danger signs: apnoea, worsening breathing, poor feeding, lethargy
Advise to return immediately if condition worsens
pathways.followUp: Review in 48 hours if symptoms persist. Return immediately if apnoea or poor feeding.

pathways.criteria: Poor feeding (50-75% normal), mild-moderate chest indrawing, tachypnoea for age, SpO2 90-94% on room air.
treatment: Admit for observation and supportive care
Oxygen via nasal prongs to keep SpO2 ≥92%
Nasal suction before feeds and PRN for congestion
Nasogastric or IV fluids if unable to feed adequately (use 80% maintenance to avoid SIADH)
Close monitoring of SpO2, RR, indrawing, feeding, and conscious level
No routine bronchodilators — trial of salbutamol ONLY if strong asthma overlap suspicion; discontinue if no objective improvement in first dose
No corticosteroids — not recommended in bronchiolitis

pathways.criteria: Any danger sign: apnoea, SpO2 <90% despite low-flow oxygen, cyanosis, unable to feed (<50%), severe indrawing, exhaustion/lethargy, grunting.
treatment: Admit to HDU/ICU
Oxygen to keep SpO2 ≥94%; escalate to high-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) if requiring >2 L/min
Consider CPAP if HFNC insufficient or rising CO2
IV fluids: 80% maintenance, monitor electrolytes (SIADH risk)
Apnoea monitoring — consider caffeine therapy if recurrent apnoea in preterm infants
No bronchodilators — per latest AAP/NICE guidelines, no role in bronchiolitis
No corticosteroids — not recommended
No antibiotics unless strong evidence of bacterial co-infection (positive culture, toxic appearance)
Prepare for mechanical ventilation if CO2 rising, exhaustion, or refractory hypoxia

hfncCriteria.startingSettings: 2 L/kg/min (max 12 L/min) with FiO2 titrated to SpO2 ≥92%
hfncCriteria.weaning: Reduce FiO2 first, then flow rate as tolerated

feedingManagement.moderate: Small frequent feeds; NG tube if <50% oral intake
feedingManagement.severe: IV fluids (80% maintenance). NBM if respiratory distress severe
feedingManagement.note: Avoid overhydration due to SIADH risk in bronchiolitis

treatmentResponseLogic.typicalCourse: Gradual improvement over 3-7 days; peak severity typically on day 3-5 of illness.

positiveResponse.criteria: Improved feeding, resolving chest indrawing, stable/improving SpO2, reduced tachypnoea.
positiveResponse.action: Continue supportive care; discharge when feeding well (≥75% normal) and SpO2 ≥90-92% off oxygen for 4-6 hours.

noResponseOrWorseningAfter24_48h.criteria: Worsening hypoxia despite HFNC, increasing work of breathing, new apnoea, persistent poor feeding.
noResponseOrWorseningAfter24_48h.action: Re-evaluate for complications (atelectasis, secondary bacterial pneumonia) and reconsider differential diagnosis: pneumonia, sepsis, cardiac failure, foreign body, aspiration.

complications.note: Especially in preterm infants and those <3 months of age
Avoid fluid overload in bronchiolitis management

disease.emergencyPriority: Apnoea, severe hypoxia, or inability to feed require immediate admission
